title: Topological Signatures of Adversaries in Multimodal Alignments
abstract: Multimodal Machine Learning systems, particularly those aligning text and image data like CLIP/BLIP models, have become increasingly prevalent, yet remain susceptible to adversarial attacks. While substantial research has addressed adversarial robustness in unimodal contexts, defense strategies for multimodal systems are underexplored. This work investigates the topological signatures that arise between image and text embeddings and shows how adversarial attacks disrupt their alignment, introducing distinctive signatures. We specifically leverage persistent homology and introduce two novel Topological- Contrastive losses based on Total Persistence and Multi-scale kernel methods to analyze the topological signatures introduced by adversarial perturbations. We observe a pattern of monotonic changes in the proposed topological losses emerging in a wide range of attacks on image-text alignments, as more adversarial samples are introduced in the data. By designing an algorithm to backpropagate these signatures to input samples, we are able to integrate these signatures into Maximum Mean Discrepancy tests, creating a novel class of tests that leverage topological signatures for better adversarial detection.
impact: This work addresses a gap in the defense of multimodal ML systems against adversarial attacks. By introducing novel Topological-Contrastive losses and leveraging persistent homology, this study not only deepens our understanding of how adversarial attacks disrupt text-image alignment but also provides practical methods to detect such disruptions with high accuracy. As Multimodal ML systems are increasingly used in sensitive domains, enabling more precise detection will enhance the security and resilience of systems that directly impact public safety, health, and trust in technology. Furthermore, the monotonic properties of the proposed losses provide interpretable insights into adversarial behavior, promoting transparency and trustworthiness in adversarial defense strategies. While this work provides powerful tools to detect and mitigate adversarial threats, it also necessitates careful ethical considerations. The methods developed here could potentially be misused to generate more advanced adversarial attacks, exacerbating the arms race between attackers and defenders. To mitigate this risk, we advocate for responsible dissemination of these techniques and call for collaboration across the research community to ensure their ethical application. Future directions include extending these topological methods to other multimodal configurations, such as video-text and audio-text systems, further solidifying the role of topological insights in enhancing the robustness of multimodal ML systems. This research demonstrates the transformative potential of topology-based approaches in securing the next generation of ML systems and sets a strong foundation for future advancements in the field.

query_input.scenario: Deployment of CLIP-based anomaly detection in autonomous systems
query_input.issue: Adversarial inputs bypass anomaly detection by preserving expected topological persistence signatures.
hazard: Adversarial perturbations exploit multimodal alignment gaps, evading detection by mimicking clean topological signatures.
exposure: Autonomous system operators relying on CLIP-based anomaly detectors

hipporag_result.vulnerability: Operators depend on anomaly detectors that cannot flag adversarial inputs mimicking clean topological persistence signatures.
hipporag_result.impact: Safety incidents and operational failures result from adversarial inputs triggering dangerous autonomous actions without operator alert.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Adversarial perturbations exploiting multimodal alignment gaps to mimic clean topological signatures, through operators relying on CLIP-based anomaly detectors, trigger dependence on detectors unable to flag such inputs, leading to safety incidents and operational failures from unalerted dangerous autonomous actions.

dose_response: Adversarial perturbations exploit multimodal alignment gaps to evade detection by mimicking clean topological signatures; in deployment of CLIP-based anomaly detection in autonomous systems, this exposes operators to undetected adversarial inputs, leading to autonomous vehicle collisions and eroded public trust.
vulnerability: Over-reliance on opaque CLIP embeddings without human-interpretable validation in safety-critical autonomous systems.
impact: Undetected adversarial inputs cause autonomous vehicle collisions, eroding public trust in AI safety.
